title: 24 Tahsas — The Eve of the Nativity & The Assembly
narrative:
The twenty-fourth day of Tahsas is the Eve of the Nativity — the vigil night before the great feast of Ganna — and the Ethiopian liturgical tradition surrounds this threshold hour with the greatest solemnity of the entire month. The Church does not begin its celebration on the feast itself but on the evening before, keeping the ancient Jewish understanding that the new day begins at sundown: the night of the twenty-fourth is already the opening movement of the twenty-fifth, and the darkness of the vigil is the darkness out of which the light of the world is about to emerge. The faithful gather in their churches throughout the night, chanting the hymns of the Incarnation, processing by torchlight, and maintaining the wakeful expectancy that befits those who wait for the coming of the Bridegroom — for the Lord has said that He comes at an hour when the household does not expect Him, and the eve of His Nativity is the supreme occasion for that attentive wakefulness.

The commemoration of the Arrival in Egypt on this day deepens the mystery of the vigil with the theme of exile and refuge. The Holy Family who fled into Egypt did not find a comfortable lodging but the hospitality of a foreign land, the poverty of displacement, the disorientation of strangers in an unknown tongue. The Egyptian tradition, which treasures every site of the Holy Family's passage, reads this arrival not as humiliation but as consecration: every place of exile, every strange land, every moment of not belonging to the comfortable world — all are sanctified by the passage of the Child whose birth this night celebrates. The Church on this vigil night identifies herself with the Holy Family: she too is a pilgrim community awaiting the fullness of a home she has not yet entered.

Saint Eugenia of Rome, who is commemorated on this day, was a noble Roman woman of the third century who disguised herself as a man, entered a monastery, rose to the position of abbot, and was eventually exposed — but in a way that revealed her innocence and led to the conversion of many, including her own family. Her name Eugenia in the Greek means 'well-born' or 'noble-born,' and the story of her disguise is interpreted by the hagiographic tradition as a parable of the soul that puts off the garments of earthly identity to take on the new identity of Christ — precisely the mystery that the feast of the Nativity proclaims, for in the Incarnation it is the divine Word who takes on the disguise of human flesh, concealing His divine glory beneath the swaddling cloths of a poor child.

The theological seal of this twenty-fourth day of Tahsas is the mystery of threshold and transformation. The vigil is itself a threshold: between the time of waiting and the time of fulfillment, between the darkness of anticipation and the light of the feast. The arrival in Egypt is a threshold: between the land of promise and the land of exile, both of which the Lord makes holy. Eugenia crosses the threshold of identity, laying down the garments of one life to take up those of another. All three together prepare the faithful for the supreme threshold of the Incarnation itself, which is crossed at the stroke of midnight between the twenty-fourth and twenty-fifth of Tahsas, when the eternal Word steps across the threshold of time and is born of the Virgin in Bethlehem.
